title: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman warns that Oklahoma Senate leadership scheduled SB495, Senator Warren Hamilton's bill to abolish abortion, for an unusually early Health and Human Services Committee hearing on February 3, 2021, in an apparent attempt to kill it before abolitionists can rally support. He calls Oklahomans to email all 13 voting members and gather outside Room 535 at 9 a.m.
audiences: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
Pro-life Christian weighing whether to take action
Curious observer of Oklahoma Senate procedure
Pastor or church leader considering attending Abolition Day

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe scheduling SB495 so early in the session is a hostile move rather than a sign of good faith?
questions.audience: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
questions.answer: Early committee hearings are unusual, and Silberman reads the timing as a deliberate attempt by leadership to kill the bill before abolitionists can mobilize lobbying or rally support around it.
questions.quote: We are praying and working for the best, but the motivation for the Senate to hear SB495 this early likely is not a good one. It is not common practice to hold committee hearings this early in the session. The objective here is to kill SB495 on day 3 of the session before we have much of a chance to lobby and rally for the bill.

questions.question: Why is the makeup of the Senate HHS Committee considered unfavorable to SB495?
questions.audience: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
questions.answer: Hamilton himself is not on the committee, neither are senators known to have supported SB13's general order vote, and nine of the eleven members previously voted to kill SB13.
questions.quote: The committee makeup is not favorable. Warren Hamilton, the author of the bill, is not on the committee, nor are Nathan Dahm, Chris Kidd, or Roland Pederson who voted in favor of placing SB13 on general order last year. The committee has 11 members. Nine of them voted to kill SB13 last year.

questions.question: What outcome does Silberman predict if the vote takes place on February 3, and what does he say leadership wants?
questions.audience: Curious observer of Oklahoma Senate procedure
questions.answer: He projects a lopsided defeat ranging from 10-3 to 13-0 against SB495, which he frames as exactly what Senate leadership wants to occur on day 3 so that the abolition push collapses before it gains momentum.
questions.quote: The likely outcome if the vote takes place tomorrow is a 10-3, 11-2, 12-1, or 13-0 vote to keep child murder legal in Oklahoma, and that's exactly what leadership wants to happen on day 3 of the session.

questions.question: Why would a defeat in committee on February 3 effectively kill abolition legislation for two years?
questions.audience: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
questions.answer: Oklahoma's legislature runs on two-year cycles, and once a bill's language is voted down in the first year, identical legislation cannot be reintroduced until the next cycle.
questions.quote: What makes this even worse is the fact that SB495 would be dead for two years, as the Oklahoma legislature operates in two-year cycles. This is the first year, and new legislation cannot be introduced between the first and second year of the cycle if the same language has already been voted down in the form of another bill.

questions.question: How is Senator Hamilton trying to keep SB495 alive in the face of an unfavorable committee vote?
questions.audience: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
questions.answer: Hamilton is exploring procedural options to delay the vote so he can whip support among colleagues, give Oklahomans more time to lobby for added coauthors, and let senators hear from the Abolition Day Rally attendees on February 9.
questions.quote: Accordingly, Senator Hamilton is exploring options to buy some time. This would allow him to whip support for the bill among his colleagues. It would give us and the people of Oklahoma more time to meet with Senators to add coauthors to the bill. It would give Senators time to hear from the Abolition Day Rally attendees on February 9th while the bill to abolish abortion is still alive.

questions.question: What is the on-the-ground call to action for Oklahoma activists on the morning of the hearing?
questions.answer: Activists are asked to gather at 9 a.m. outside Room 535 to line the halls as senators arrive and then attend the hearing inside, regardless of whether SB495 is actually heard that day.
questions.quote: So the bill may or may not be heard tomorrow. Regardless of whether it is heard, we will be meeting outside the committee hearing room (room 535) at 9 a.m. to make our presence felt.

questions.question: What action does Silberman ask Oklahomans who cannot attend in person to take?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian weighing whether to take action
questions.answer: He urges them to email every member of the HHS Committee asking for a yes vote on SB495, and provides the full list of senator email addresses.
questions.quote: It is EXTREMELY important that Oklahomans email all members of the committee asking them to vote in favor of the bill in case the bill is heard tomorrow.

questions.question: Why does the committee include Greg Treat and Kim David even though they are not regular HHS members?
questions.audience: Curious observer of Oklahoma Senate procedure
questions.answer: As Senate Pro Tem and Floor Leader respectively, Treat and David are voting members of every committee, which gives them the ability to cast votes against SB495 on top of the standard eleven members.
questions.quote: Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat and Floor Leader Kim David are voting members of every committee, so they also are able to vote against SB495.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the two freshman senators on the committee?
questions.audience: Legislative strategist following state-level abolition bills
questions.answer: He labels Jo Anna Dossett a pro-abortion Democrat certain to vote no, and says Jessica Garvin has not gone on record either way but communications with her have not been promising.
questions.quote: Freshman Senator Jo Anna Dossett is a pro-child murder Democrat, so she will vote against it. Freshman Senator Jessica Garvin has never gone on record for or against abolishing abortion like the other members of the committee, but our communication with her thus far has not been promising.

related.title: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
related.reason: Promotes the same February 9 Abolition Day Rally that Hamilton is trying to keep SB495 alive long enough for senators to hear.

related.title: OK Senate HHS Committee Votes in Favor of Murdering 10,000 Babies
related.reason: Reports the actual outcome of the very HHS committee vote this article anticipates.

related.title: Though they intended evil against You and devised a plot, they will not succeed.
related.reason: Aaron Hoffman's follow-up reflection on the SB495 defeat in the same HHS committee meeting.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Documents prior precedent of HHS Chair Greg McCortney blocking abolition legislation, the playbook being repeated against SB495.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Background on Pro Tem Greg Treat, named in this article as one of the leadership figures positioned to vote against SB495.

related.title: Warren Hamilton Wins!
related.reason: Covers Warren Hamilton's election to the Oklahoma Senate, the prerequisite for him being able to author SB495.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Explains the abolitionist single-bill strategy that SB495 continues, including why a no vote in committee would shut down identical language for the cycle.
